q: Correct order for reprocessing instruments with stuck organic debris?
options: Sterilization → cleaning → disinfection
Cleaning → disinfection → sterilization
Sterilization → disinfection → cleaning
Disinfection → cleaning → sterilization
explanation: Debris must be cleaned first; then disinfect; then sterilize. Dirty instruments must not go straight into sterilizer.

q: Dental light handles between patients should be:
options: Barrier-protected and/or disinfected each patient
Wiped only once a month
Autoclaved as a whole unit daily only
Never cleaned
explanation: Clinical contact surfaces: barrier or intermediate-level disinfection per patient.

q: Purpose of PPE in dentistry primarily is to:
options: Replace hand hygiene completely
Protect DHCP and patients by reducing exposure to infectious materials
Sterilize instruments inside the pack
Increase aerosol production intentionally
explanation: PPE is a barrier; hand hygiene remains essential.

q: Hand hygiene is considered:
options: Optional if gloves are worn
Replaced entirely by mask use
A cornerstone of infection prevention before/after patient contact and glove use
Only needed at end of day
explanation: Gloves do not replace hand hygiene.

q: Critical instruments (enter sterile tissue / bloodstream) require:
options: Only soap wash
Only wipe with alcohol between patients
Heat sterilization (or sterile single-use)
No processing if they look clean
explanation: Spaulding: critical → sterilize.

q: Semi-critical items that touch mucous membranes ideally:
options: No cleaning needed
Store wet in drawer
Heat sterilized; if heat-sensitive, high-level disinfection minimum
Only low-level wipe always enough
explanation: Semi-critical: sterilize preferred; HLD if heat-sensitive.

q: Noncritical environmental surfaces (e.g., countertops) generally need:
options: Only sterile surgical drapes always
Cleaning and intermediate/low-level disinfection as appropriate
Autoclave the entire counter daily
No cleaning ever
explanation: Noncritical: clean ± disinfect; not sterilization.

q: Steam autoclave biological indicator (spore test) is used to:
options: Test fluoride concentration
Replace chemical indicators completely without ever using them
Verify sterilizer kill efficacy with highly resistant spores
Measure room temperature only
explanation: BI is gold standard monitoring; chemical indicators are adjuncts.

q: Chemical indicator on a pouch turning color means:
options: Item was exposed to sterilizing conditions parameters of that indicator — not a guarantee of sterility alone
Instrument is contaminated
Absolute proof of sterility equal to biologic always
Autoclave is broken always
explanation: Chemical ≠ biologic; use both in a monitoring program.

q: Needle-stick injury first immediate action?
options: Continue working without reporting
Squeeze wound aggressively and apply bleach inside
Ignore if patient looks healthy
Wash wound with soap and water; report and follow occupational exposure protocol
explanation: Wash, report, risk assess source, PEP as indicated.

q: After needle-stick from known HIV source, PEP should generally start:
options: As soon as possible (hours matter); follow institutional protocol
After 2 weeks only
Only if symptoms appear
Never indicated conceptually
explanation: Post-exposure prophylaxis is time-sensitive.

q: Multi-dose vial best practice includes:
options: Use same syringe for multiple patients if needle changed only
Leave needle in septum between patients
Store at room temperature forever unlabeled
Aseptic access, date when opened, discard per policy; dedicate when possible
explanation: Unsafe injection practices transmit bloodborne pathogens.

q: Sharps disposal should be into:
options: Puncture-resistant labeled sharps container at point of use
Patient's pocket
Sterilizer chamber loose
Regular trash bag
explanation: Never recap with two hands; dispose immediately in sharps box.

q: Informed consent requires that the patient understands:
options: Nothing if emergency is not present and doctor decides alone always
Only the cost without risks
Only the brand of composite
Nature of procedure, risks, benefits, alternatives, and can decide voluntarily
explanation: Consent = information + capacity + voluntariness.

q: A minor typically needs consent from:
options: Parent/legal guardian for elective care (exceptions: emergencies / local law mature minor rules)
Classmate only
No one ever
Any adult in waiting room without authority
explanation: Know local SCFHS/KSA rules; guardian consent for minors generally.

q: Patient refuses a recommended extraction of a hopeless tooth. Best ethical response?
options: Extract anyway under force
Explain risks of refusal, document informed refusal, offer alternatives/follow-up — do not coerce
Refuse any future emergency care forever as punishment
Falsify chart that extraction was done
explanation: Respect autonomy; document informed refusal.

q: Confidentiality means:
options: Discuss full cases with names on public social media freely
Patient information is not disclosed without authorization except legal/ethical exceptions
Share records with employers without consent always
Post treatment photos of celebrities for advertising without consent
explanation: Privacy is an ethical and legal duty.

q: Non-maleficence in dental ethics means:
options: Ignore infection control
Always maximize clinic profit first
Do no harm — avoid unnecessary risk and negligence
Treat only family members
explanation: Primum non nocere — core principle.

q: Beneficence means:
options: Only do what is easiest for the dentist
Disclose secrets of other patients
Avoid all treatment always
Act in the patient's best interest to promote good oral health outcomes
explanation: Beneficence: promote well-being.

q: Justice in healthcare ethics relates to:
options: Ignoring disability access
Fairness in distribution of care and non-discrimination
Charging different races different quality intentionally
Only treating wealthy patients
explanation: Justice: equitable treatment.

q: Treating a famous patient then posting identifiable photos online without consent is a breach of:
options: Confidentiality / privacy (and often advertising ethics rules)
Only instrument sterilization rules
Nothing wrong ever
Only fluoride guidelines
explanation: No public disclosure of identifiable patient info without consent.

q: Recent organ transplant (e.g., 3 months) needing elective restoration with gingivitis — best approach?
options: Only extract all teeth immediately without consult
Do full mouth implants same day without labs
Ignore medical history
Defer elective care; coordinate with physician; manage infection risk carefully when treated
explanation: Immunosuppression + meds (cyclosporine → overgrowth) → defer elective; medical clearance.

q: Preferred analgesic concept in asthmatic patient with NSAID-sensitivity concern?
options: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) as safer first-line analgesic often
High-dose aspirin always first
Codeine only option always
Ibuprofen always mandatory
explanation: NSAID-exacerbated respiratory disease risk — prefer paracetamol.

q: COPD patient for routine dentistry — positioning/sedation approach?
options: Semi-supine; short appointments; cautious anxiolysis avoiding heavy respiratory depression
No modifications ever
Trendelenburg + high-dose diazepam always
Fully flat + deep IV sedation first-line always
explanation: COPD: upright/semi-upright; protect ventilation.

q: Uncontrolled hyperthyroid patient — local anesthetic with epinephrine concern?
options: Epinephrine is completely irrelevant
Only treat with general anesthesia always without medical eval
Use caution / medical optimization; avoid excess epinephrine that may precipitate crisis patterns
Always use maximum epi carpules without limit
explanation: Hyperthyroid: arrhythmia/crisis risk; coordinate care; careful vasoconstrictor use.

q: Patient on warfarin for simple extraction — common modern approach concept?
options: Always stop warfarin 2 weeks without physician always
Often continue anticoagulant if INR in therapeutic range and use local hemostatic measures (physician coordinate)
Always hospitalize for every simple extraction without exception
Ignore INR completely
explanation: Risk of thrombosis vs bleeding — often continue VKA if INR acceptable + local control.

q: Drug-induced gingival enlargement classic drugs include:
options: Fluoride varnish only
Nifedipine (CCBs), phenytoin, cyclosporine
Paracetamol only
Amoxicillin only
explanation: Three classics: CCB, phenytoin, cyclosporine.

q: Renal transplant patient with gingival overgrowth on multiple meds — most likely culprit among antihypertensives?
options: Thyroxine only
Calcium channel blocker (e.g., nifedipine) and/or cyclosporine from immunosuppression
Hydrochlorothiazide only always
Insulin only
explanation: CCB + cyclosporine both cause enlargement — exam favorite.

q: Bisphosphonate / denosumab patient needing extraction — key concern?
options: MRONJ risk — prefer prevention, atraumatic technique, informed consent, medical coordination
No special risk ever
Always immediately place immediate implant without discussion
Only risk is fluorosis
explanation: MRONJ: antiresorptives + trauma/infection risk factors.

q: Pregnancy — elective extensive care timing often preferred:
options: Second trimester when possible; emergency care any trimester with precautions
Never treat dental pain in pregnancy
Only first trimester for all x-rays and surgery elective always preferred
Only postpartum for any emergency abscess
explanation: 2nd trimester preferred for elective; emergencies don't wait.

q: Radiographs in pregnancy when clinically necessary:
options: Use when benefit outweighs risk with proper shielding — do not withhold needed diagnostic films
No lead apron ever
Double the exposure intentionally
Never take any radiograph even for abscess
explanation: ALARA + indicated imaging is acceptable with protection.

q: Infective endocarditis prophylaxis — modern concept:
options: Only for complete denture delivery
Only for specific high-risk cardiac conditions and specified procedures — not universal
Never for any dental procedure ever
For every filling in all healthy patients
explanation: Guidelines narrowed prophylaxis indications — know principles.

q: Patient with unstable angina scheduled for elective crown prep today — best action?
options: Defer elective care; medical evaluation/optimization first
Proceed with long stressful appointment and high epi load
Extract all teeth same day
General anesthesia in office without clearance always
explanation: Unstable cardiac disease → defer elective dentistry.

q: Uncontrolled diabetes (very high glucose / unstable) before elective implant?
options: Ignore HbA1c
Place full-arch immediate implants same day always
Only increase sugar intake pre-op
Improve control / coordinate physician; prefer reversible options until stable
explanation: Poor control impairs healing/infection risk — especially implants.

q: Asthma patient brings inhaler — dental appointment advice?
options: Lie fully flat with heavy sedation first line
Use aspirin premed always
Have rescue inhaler available; reduce stress; consider late morning appointments if nocturnal symptoms
Confiscate inhaler
explanation: Prevention of bronchospasm: inhaler access, stress control.

q: Latex allergy in dental clinic — action?
options: Use more latex powder intentionally
Ignore history
Only cotton gloves for surgery always without barrier alternatives
Latex-free environment / nitrile gloves; flag chart
explanation: Avoid latex exposure completely for allergic patients.

q: HIV patient requesting routine care — ethical/clinical stance?
options: Refuse all HIV patients always
Provide care with standard precautions; may request recent labs for complex invasive planning when relevant — no unjustified refusal
Use no gloves to show trust
Announce HIV status to waiting room
explanation: Standard precautions for all; non-discrimination; clinical labs when procedure-relevant.

q: Standard precautions mean:
options: No need for PPE if patient is family
Assume all blood/body fluids potentially infectious — apply to all patients
Only apply PPE to patients who look ill
Only sterilize when visible blood
explanation: Standard precautions are universal baseline.

q: After prophy cup single-use disposable type is used, correct handling?
options: Dispose as single-use; do not reprocess for another patient
Give to patient to reuse at home as sterile surgical tool
Wash and autoclave and reuse always if rubber
Store uncleaned for next patient
explanation: Single-use items are single-use.

q: Impression disinfection before lab send-out:
options: Soak in pure glutaraldehyde for days destroying cast accuracy always without protocol
Autoclave alginate impression
Rinse, disinfect per material-compatible intermediate-level protocol, rinse as directed
Send dripping with saliva without disinfection
explanation: Disinfect impressions; follow material instructions.

q: Dental unit waterlines should deliver water meeting:
options: Only sterile water never needed for surgery and never for anything
No monitoring ever
Any stagnant biofilm-rich water is fine
Potable-quality microbial standards; purge/treat waterlines per protocol
explanation: DUWL biofilm control is required infection control element.

q: When is autoclave load considered not released?
options: If chemical/mechanical monitors fail or BI fails — do not use instruments until resolved
Color of pouch never matters and BI never used
Only check once a year
Always release even if BI positive
explanation: Failed monitoring → quarantine load, reprocess, investigate.

q: Truthfulness (veracity) requires the dentist to:
options: Hide all risks always
Alter records to hide errors
Guarantee outcomes that cannot be guaranteed
Provide honest information about diagnosis and treatment options
explanation: Veracity underpins informed consent.

q: Error disclosure ethical approach after wrong-tooth near miss caught before prep?
options: Blame only the patient
Delete the chart
Hide from patient and staff forever
Disclose appropriately, apologize as indicated, analyze system, document — improve safety
explanation: Culture of safety: transparency + learning.

q: Patient offers cash to falsify sick leave dental certificate without treatment — response?
options: Refuse; fraudulent certification is unethical and illegal
Accept cash and write false certificate
Ask for more money
Have assistant sign instead to avoid responsibility
explanation: Honesty in documentation is mandatory.

q: Scope of practice / referral duty when case exceeds skill?
options: Refer to appropriate specialist rather than attempt harmful care
Attempt complex surgery without training to save fee
Abandon patient without referral options
Only treat if cash paid double
explanation: Non-maleficence + competence boundaries.

q: Emergency patient with airway-threatening infection after hours — duty?
options: Only book in 6 months
Refuse all emergencies always
Provide/arrange emergency care; do not abandon
Send home without advice if not your patient of record ever without pathway
explanation: Emergency care / appropriate transfer is ethical duty.

q: Child suspected abuse (non-accidental trauma) — professional duty concept?
options: Confront parent aggressively in public only
Document carefully and report per legal mandatory reporting pathways
Ignore to avoid paperwork
Post photos on social media
explanation: Mandatory reporting laws protect children — know local process.

q: Capacity assessment for consent is questionable when patient is:
options: Intoxicated, severely cognitively impaired, or unable to understand/retain/weigh information
Asking questions about risks
Choosing less expensive option
Requesting a second opinion
explanation: Capacity is decision-specific; questions ≠ incapacity.

q: Working while knowingly having highly infectious active influenza with fever — ethical infection control action?
options: Do not provide patient care until fit; avoid exposing patients/staff
Only treat immunocompromised patients that day
Hide symptoms and continue AGPs without precautions
Work without mask and cough freely
explanation: Presenteeism harms patients — stay out when infectious.
